title: Please write essay that explore how Samsung executes various price and non-price strategies in an attempt to establish a…

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Please write essay that explore how Samsung executes various price and non-price strategies in an attempt to establish and maintain a competitive advantage in the competition with Apple. In so doing, illustrate the inter-dependencies via an action-reaction sequence of events. 
Your essay should critically 1. assess the value-added of transaction cost economics and game theory tools. 
2. outline the overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts introduced in this Module. 
3. illustrate how the transaction cost economics and the economics of game theory can be used to reshape strategy. 
The best way to approach the assignment is to focus on concerns may have to do with (i) market entry (ii) product launch (iii) challenges of technology or (iv) prices. So, 1. proceed to analyse the market-asa-game, 2. identify and filter the competitive threat, 3. assess the action-reaction sequence of events and 4. evaluate your results. And always 5. provide a risk assessment of the likely competitive threats facing a company on choice of strategy.

Please cite the following articles:
Akshay R., R. (2000). How to Fight a Price War. Harvard Business Review,.
Bill, G. (2014). How to Outsmart Activist Investors. Harvard Business Review,.
Dan, A. (2009). The End of Rational Economics. Harvard Business Review, 87(7).
Feng, Z. (2019). Why some platforms thrive… and others don’t . Harvard business review,.
Hanna, H. (2014). THE LIMITS OF SCALE. Harvard Business Review,.
Kevin. P, C. (2009). Predicting Your Competitor’s Reaction. Harvard Business Review,.
Oclc.org. (2023). Shibboleth Authentication Request. [online] Available at: https://web-s-ebscohost-com.manchester.idm.oclc.org/ehost/pdfviewer/pdfviewer?vid=0&sid=8aa99285-401d-4ba4-93a2-9b09841bdaa5%40redis [Accessed 18 May 2023].
Rao, A.R. (2000). How to Fight a Price War. Harvard Business Review,.
Tang, C.S. (2010). A review of marketing–operations interface models: From co-existence to coordination and collaboration. International Journal of Production Economics, 125(1), pp.22–40. doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpe.2010.01.014.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
In the competitive smartphone industry, Samsung and Apple have dominated the market as major players, with Samsung implementing various price and non-price strategies to establish and maintain a competitive advantage over Apple. Transaction cost economics and game theory tools can aid in analyzing Samsung's strategic decisions and the potential outcome of these decisions.

To enter the market, Samsung had to analyze and identify the competitive threats from established players like Apple. Game theory can assist in evaluating competitors' strategies and predicting their reactions to Samsung's product launches or pricing decisions. For instance, Samsung launched the Galaxy S20 Ultra with a 108-megapixel camera to compete with Apple's iPhone 11 Pro and Pro Max. This launch can initiate an action-reaction sequence of events where Apple may introduce a new feature or reduce prices of its product to maintain its market position.

Samsung has managed to establish a competitive advantage over Apple by providing a variety of product offerings at different price points, making it accessible to a broader customer base. The overlap between micro-economics and game theory concepts suggests that Samsung's pricing strategy represents price discrimination, whereby Samsung can charge different prices for the same product, depending on the customer's willingness to pay. For instance, Samsung's Galaxy S21 smartphone retails at $799, while the premium Galaxy S21 Ultra 5G sells at $1199.

Transaction cost economics can aid in evaluating the costs associated with Samsung's pricing strategies. Samsung can benefit from economies of scale by producing a wide range of smartphones, thereby reducing transaction costs. However, Samsung needs to execute strategies that provide customers with value, as minimizing transaction costs can be accompanied by consumer exploitation. In addition, Samsung's non-price strategies such as advertising and brand image play a crucial role in establishing a long-term competitive advantage over Apple.

As Samsung faces challenges of technology, it needs to adopt strategies that focus on innovation and technological advancements. Samsung's partnership with Google to develop an OS that can rival Apple's iOS demonstrates a strategy that aims to provide a superior user experience, making Samsung a formidable competitor in the smartphone industry.

In conclusion, Samsung has established a competitive advantage over Apple by implementing various price and non-price strategies. Transaction cost economics and game theory tools can assist in analyzing Samsung's strategic decisions and predicting potential outcomes. Samsung's success depends on its ability to continually innovate and provide superior products and services that offer customers value. However, Samsung needs to continually assess and evaluate the potential risks of its strategies to ensure its sustainable growth in the smartphone industry.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
In the highly competitive smartphone industry, Samsung and Apple have been competing for years to establish and maintain a dominant market position. Samsung has implemented a variety of price and non-price strategies to try to gain an advantage over Apple. This essay will explore these strategies and how they interrelate in the context of market entry, product launch, challenges of technology, and prices. The essay will also critically assess the value-added of transaction cost economics and game theory tools, the overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts, and how the transaction cost economics and the economics of game theory can be used to reshape strategy.

To enter the market, Samsung had to analyze and identify the competitive threats from established players like Apple. Game theory tools can help in evaluating competitors' strategies and predicting their reactions to Samsung's product launches or pricing decisions. For instance, when Samsung launched the Galaxy S4 and S5 smartphones, Apple responded by releasing new features for its iPhones. Similarly, when Samsung introduced the Galaxy Note Edge and Galaxy S6 Edge smartphones, Apple counteracted by offering larger screen sizes in its iPhone 6 and 6 Plus.

Samsung's pricing strategy represents price discrimination, whereby Samsung can charge different prices for the same product, depending on the customer's willingness to pay. This strategy is anchored on the overlaps between microeconomics and game theoretic concepts. Transaction cost economics also plays a significant role in Samsung's pricing strategies. Samsung needs to execute strategies that provide customers with value, as minimizing transaction costs can be accompanied by consumer exploitation.

In terms of technology challenges, Samsung has adopted strategies focusing on innovation and technological advancements. For instance, when Apple launched its Siri virtual assistant, Samsung introduced Bixby as a rival. Similarly, Samsung's partnership with Google to develop an OS that can rival Apple's iOS demonstrates a strategy that aims to provide a superior user experience, making Samsung a formidable competitor in the smartphone industry.

Samsung's non-price strategies, such as advertising and brand image, play a crucial role in establishing a long-term competitive advantage over Apple. Samsung's brand identity is built on innovation, innovation, and innovation. Samsung has utilized various advertising channels to promote its products, including social media, public events, and Samsung stores. This strategy ensures that Samsung keeps its customers updated on new and innovative products.

In conclusion, Samsung has managed to establish a competitive advantage over Apple through various price and non-price strategies. The interdependence of these strategies can be analyzed using transaction cost economics and game theory tools. Samsung's success relies on its ability to continually innovate and provide superior products and services that offer customers value. Samsung needs to continually assess and evaluate the potential risks of its strategies to ensure its sustainable growth in the smartphone industry.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
In the fiercely competitive smartphone industry, Samsung and Apple have been battling for market dominance for years. Samsung has implemented various price and non-price strategies to try to establish and maintain a competitive advantage over Apple. This essay will examine these strategies and their interdependence in the context of market entry, product launch, technology challenges, and pricing. The essay will also evaluate the value-added of transaction cost economics and game theory tools, outline the overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts, and illustrate how these concepts can be used to reshape strategy.

To enter the market, Samsung had to analyze and identify the competitive threats from established players like Apple. Game theory tools are useful in evaluating competitors' strategies and predicting their reactions to Samsung's product launches or pricing decisions. Samsung's introduction of the Galaxy S21 smartphone at a lower price point had several reactions from Apple. To maintain its market position, Apple launched an updated iPhone model with improved features. This launch starts an action-reaction sequence of events where Samsung may introduce a new feature or reduce prices of its product to retain its market position.

Samsung has managed to establish a competitive advantage over Apple by providing a variety of product offerings at different price points. This strategy represents price discrimination, whereby Samsung can charge different prices for the same product, depending on the customer's willingness to pay. The overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts suggests that this pricing strategy optimizes the revenue for Samsung while satisfying the customers' demands.

To evaluate the costs associated with Samsung's pricing strategies, transaction cost economics concepts are useful. Samsung can benefit from economies of scale by producing a wide range of smartphones, thereby reducing transaction costs. However, minimizing transaction costs can be accompanied by consumer exploitation. Therefore, Samsung needs to execute strategies that provide customers with value.

In terms of technology challenges, Samsung needs to adopt strategies that focus on innovation and technological advancements. For instance, Samsung introduced Bixby as a rival to Apple's Siri virtual assistant. Collaborating with Google to develop an OS that can rival Apple's iOS is another strategy that aims to provide a superior user experience, making Samsung a formidable competitor in the smartphone industry.

Samsung's non-price strategies, such as advertising and brand image, play a significant role in establishing a long-term competitive advantage over Apple. Samsung has utilized several advertising channels to promote its products, including social media, public events, and Samsung stores. This strategy ensures that Samsung keeps its customers updated on new and innovative products, creating a perception of innovation, versatility, and style.

In conclusion, Samsung has implemented various price and non-price strategies to try to maintain a competitive advantage over Apple. The interdependence of these strategies can be analyzed using transaction cost economics and game theory tools. Samsung's success depends on its ability to continually innovate and provide superior products and services that offer customers value. Samsung needs to continually assess and evaluate the potential risks of its strategies to ensure its sustainable growth in the smartphone industry.
